claim: The Voynich script is a semi-syllabic system derived from Balkan scribal traditions, where complex glyphs are built from base characters plus modifiers (loops, triangles). The giant P glyph = N (known Balkan value), with loop = NO. The 4-shaped glyph = D, with triangle = DN, with loop = DNO. The g-shaped glyph = je/j/ja/ju. Combined glyphs produce Serbo-Croatian words (e.g., g + modified-P = jedan/jedno meaning 'one').
evidence_for: AnnaLisetteMorris2: P-glyph = N matches known Balkan scribal system values
Glyph modifier logic (base + loop/triangle) is consistent with semi-syllabic encoding
Balkan origin is historically plausible (15th century manuscript, possible Dalmatian/Bosnian provenance)
Semi-syllabic nature would explain Voynich word-length anomalies (words are shorter than expected for alphabetic cipher)
Compatible with Brain-V finding that glyph y is a grammatical marker not a letter
evidence_against: Brain-V IC of 0.077 is above Serbo-Croatian expected range (~0.060-0.065)
Brain-V frequency correlation favours Latin/Italian over Slavic languages
Entropy 3.863 bits closer to Latin (4.0) than South Slavic (~4.1-4.3)
No published systematic decipherment of multiple folios using this system
Semi-syllabic encoding would produce different word-length distribution than observed
Brain-V universal cipher transfer test shows one substitution table across all sections — a syllabic system would show more section variation
test: Test Balkan syllabic hypothesis: (1) Check if Voynich glyph combinatorics match semi-syllabic structure (base glyphs + modifier frequency). (2) Compare word-length distribution to Serbo-Croatian. (3) Test if proposed glyph values (P=N, 4=D, g=je) produce consistent readings across multiple folios. (4) Compare IC to Serbo-Croatian reference.
test_threshold: Glyph modifier patterns producing consistent SC readings on >2 folios would support

tests_run.test: Frequency correlation and IC vs Serbo-Croatian
tests_run.details: SC freq corr=0.4252, IC=0.07711 closer to Latin than SC.

Zipfian distribution (0.8946) and word length (5.03) match Serbo-Croatian expectations, supporting the natural language hypothesis. However, the unusually high hapax ratio (0.701) contradicts typical natural language patterns, and the specific glyph value claims (P=N, 4=D, g=je) cannot be validated against the corpus statistics provided. Inconclusive without folio-level mapping verification and Index of Coincidence comparison.

Could not evaluate: Test Balkan syllabic hypothesis: (1) Check if Voynich glyph combinatorics match semi-syllabic structure (base glyphs + modifier frequency). (2) Compare word-length distribution to Serbo-Croatian. (3) Test if proposed glyph values (P=N, 4=D, g=je) produce consistent readings across multiple folios. (4) Compare IC to Serbo-Croatian reference.

Statistics show word-length (5.03) and Zipf exponent consistent with natural language, but high hapax ratio (0.701) and lack of empirical validation of proposed glyph-to-Serbo-Croatian mappings make the hypothesis inconclusive rather than supported.

Statistics confirm natural-language-like properties (Zipfian distribution, hapax ratio) but provide no evidence for the specific Balkan syllabic system, proposed glyph values, or Serbo-Croatian word generation; hypothesis lacks empirical validation beyond speculation.

General statistics show natural language traits (Zipf â‰ˆ1.0), but 70% hapax ratio contradicts efficient semi-syllabic encoding, and specific glyph mappings (P=N, 4=D, g=je) lack validation across folios.
Zipfian distribution and word length suggest plausibility, but 70.1% hapax ratio contradicts natural Serbo-Croatian structure and glyph mappings lack cross-folio verification.

reasoning: Community submission from Reddit user AnnaLisetteMorris2. Proposes a Balkan semi-syllabic system where complex Voynich glyphs are composed of base characters plus modifiers (loops, triangles), producing South Slavic (Serbo-Croatian) readings. The modifier logic is an interesting structural claim that could be tested against glyph decomposition analysis. Related to H104 (Stoilova Moravian) in proposing a Slavic language but differs in the specific mechanism (syllabic vs alphabetic) and language family branch (South vs West Slavic).
